programs.description: Ticking clock — each cycle prints eight X's followed by a 'y'. We verify the first couple of cycles.
Counts up from 1 — the simplest of Benito's counter programs.
Counter written in Benito's poetic Homespring style.
Poetic counter with additional filler words — takes longer to start emitting.
Elaborate counter built from digit generators — prints an underscore ramp first.
Counter variant — prints underscores then an x-prefixed alphabet ramp.

Large counter (~10 KB of source) — emits a '0
1
2
…' sequence with no stdin.
FizzBuzz — Fizz for multiples of 3, Buzz for 5, FizzBuzz for both. Slow to start on homespring.js.
FizzBuzz in the poetic style — considerably longer source than fizzbuzz.hs.
Compact FizzBuzz driven by a time node — emits tick markers between the numbers.
Benito's 'Hello World' — emitted as 'hello world' by the complete interpreters.
Compact Hello World — the example shipped with Cal's Perl interpreter.
Single-digit adder — pipes two digits through a unary counter. Famously slow.
One-line cat — an empty/newline program that echoes stdin to stdout.
Infinite spring — repeatedly emits 'Hello, world.' until interrupted. We cap ticks and verify a prefix.
Hello World #1 — minimal hatchery + bear + powers + marshy + snowmelt.
Hello World #2 — variation with extra filler words between the meaningful tokens.
Hello World #3 — uses a hydro power + spring source, force field, shallows, and sense nodes.
Interactive greeter — asks 'What's your name?' and responds 'Hi, <name>!'.
Prints the HOMESPRING acronym — an acrostic program with no canonical output.
Zero-byte program — the null program is specially trapped and is not a quine.
Quiz — asks 'what's six times four?' and validates the answer. '24' terminates silently; other input yields 'you lie!'.
Trivial one-byte program (a lone newline): parses to a tree with no active nodes; produces no output.
Flip-flop toggle using an inverse-lock and a pair of pump/switch nodes.
Tic-tac-toe — needs stdin input to start. Prints the board frames between moves.
Reverse — uses force.up and split. Every interpreter produces a different output.
Reverse variant — 'split wings calm the ebb and flow'.
Reverse — third variation of the arrangement.
Splits input into pieces. We can't verify expected output automatically.

notes: Jeff's interpreter loops 'o world' indefinitely
Perl module does not support the node types used

Jeff's Guile interpreter runs this program correctly under an interactive terminal — typing `2` then `3` at the prompts produces `? + 5` — but the harness's one-shot piped stdin closes before the slow unary counter finishes. Count this as a pass-in-spirit.
Perl module cannot handle the full add program

Jeff's Guile interpreter does echo stdin (verify with `yes hello | hs cat.hs`), but it requires a continuously-open stdin stream; the harness's piped single-shot input closes too quickly for the interpreter's poll-based reader to pick up the data. Count as pass-in-spirit.
Perl module has no input support
